records.ety: [OE. scorn, scarn, scharn, OF. escarn, escharn, eschar, of German origin; cf. OHG. skern mockery, skernōn to mock; but cf. also OF. escorner to mock.]

defs: Extreme and lofty contempt; haughty disregard; that disdain which springs from the opinion of the utter meanness and unworthiness of an object.
quotes: Scorn at first makes after love the more.
And wandered backward as in scorn, To wait an aeon to be born.

defs: An act or expression of extreme contempt.
quotes: Every sullen frown and bitter scorn But fanned the fuel that too fast did burn.

defs: An object of extreme disdain, contempt, or derision.
quotes: Thou makest us a reproach to our neighbors, a scorn and a derision to them that are round about us.

records.ety: [OE. scornen, scarnen, schornen, OF. escarnir, escharnir. See Scorn, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Scorned (skrnd); p. pr. & vb. n. Scoring.]

defs: To hold in extreme contempt; to reject as unworthy of regard; to despise; to contemn; to disdain.
quotes: I scorn thy meat; 't would choke me.
This my long sufferance, and my day of grace, Those who neglect and scorn shall never taste.
We scorn what is in itself contemptible or disgraceful.

defs: To treat with extreme contempt; to make the object of insult; to mock; to scoff at; to deride.
quotes: His fellow, that lay by his bed's side, Gan for to laugh, and scorned him full fast.
To taunt and scorn you thus opprobriously.

defs: To scoff; to mock; to show contumely, derision, or reproach; to act disdainfully.
quotes: He said mine eyes were black and my hair black, And, now I am remembered, scorned at me.
